name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّهُۥ عَلَيْكَ ۖ مِنْهَا قَآئِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ (١٠٠)
```

```english
These are from the accounts of the townships, which We recount to you. Of them, there are some that still stand, and some that have been mown down. (100)
```

```arabic
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ فَمَآ أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ ءَالِهَتُهُمُ ٱلَّتِى يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ لَّمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا زَادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ (١٠١)
```

```english
We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves. When your Lord’s edict came, their gods whom they would invoke besides Allah were of no avail to them in any wise, and they did not increase them in anything but ruin. (101)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذَآ أَخَذَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَهِىَ ظَـٰلِمَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُۥٓ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٠٢)
```

```english
Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the townships that are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe. (102)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّمَنْ خَافَ عَذَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّشْهُودٌ (١٠٣)
```

```english
There is indeed a sign in that for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a day on which all mankind will be gathered, and it is a day witnessed [by all creatures]. (103)
```

```arabic
وَمَا نُؤَخِّرُهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَّعْدُودٍ (١٠٤)
```

```english
And We do not defer it but for a determinate term. (104)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لَا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِىٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ (١٠٥)
```

```english
The day it comes, no one shall speak except by His leave. [On that day,] some of them will be wretched and [some] felicitous. (105)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ شَقُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلنَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ (١٠٦)
```

```english
As for the wretched, they shall be in the Fire: their lot therein will be groaning and wailing. (106)
```

```arabic
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (١٠٧)
```

```english
They shall remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish; indeed your Lord does whatever He desires. (107)
```

```arabic
۞ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا۟ فَفِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ ۖ عَطَآءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (١٠٨)
```

```english
As for the felicitous, they will be in paradise. They will remain in it for as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty. (108)
```

## (Commentary)

In these verses, there is a return to the preceding stories from a universal perspective (بمنظر كلي) that summarizes the custom (سنة) of Allah regarding His servants, and the consequence of polytheism (الشرك) in unjust nations—namely, destruction in this world and eternal punishment in the Hereafter—so that those who take heed may find a lesson therein.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْقُرى نَقُصُّهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنْها قائِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ **"That is from the news of the towns which We relate to you; of them some are standing and some mown down" (11:100).** The word "that" (ذلك) refers to the stories mentioned previously. The preposition "from" (من) is partitive (تبعيضية), meaning: that which We have related to you is some of the news of the cities and lands, or their inhabitants, which We relate to you.

His saying: مِنْها قائِمٌ وَحَصِيدٌ **"of them some are standing and some mown down."** Harvesting (الحصد) is the cutting of crops; He likened the towns to crops that are either standing (قائم) or harvested (حصيد). The meaning—if "towns" refers to the physical places themselves—is that among the towns whose news We have related to you are those that are still standing, whose remaining traces that point to them have not entirely vanished, such as the towns of the people of Lot at the time their story was revealed in the Quran, as He said: وَلَقَدْ تَرَكْنا مِنْها آيَةً بَيِّنَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ **"Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for a people who exercise their reason" (29:35)**, and He said: وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَبِالَّيْلِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ **"Indeed you pass by them at dawn and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?" (37:137-138).** And among them are those whose traces have been erased and whose landmarks have been obliterated, such as the towns of the people of Noah and ‘Ad.

If "towns" refers to their inhabitants, then the meaning is that among those nations and generations are those who are "standing," whose root was not entirely cut off, such as the nations of Noah and Salih; and among them are those whose root Allah cut off, such as the people of Lot, from whom none were saved except the household of Lot, and Lot was not originally of them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَلكِنْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ **"We did not wrong them, but they wronged themselves" (11:101)** to the end of the verse. That is, We did not wrong them in sending down the punishment upon them and destroying them following their polytheism (الشرك) and transgression (الفسوق); rather, they wronged themselves when they associated partners with Allah and departed from the state of servitude (زي العبودية). Whenever there is an act and a penalty for it, one of the two must be an injustice (ظلم)—either the act or the penalty. Since the penalty was not an injustice, the injustice was the act itself which necessitated the penalty.

The gist of the matter is that We punished them because of their injustice; therefore, He followed it with His saying: فَمَا أَغْنَتْ عَنْهُمْ آلِهَتُهُمُ **"so their gods... did not avail them" (11:101)** etc. This is because the logical structure is: We seized them, and their gods did not avail them. Thus, the consequence is derived from what is indicated by His saying: وَما ظَلَمْناهُمْ **"We did not wrong them"** etc. The meaning is: We seized them, and their gods—whom they used to invoke besides Allah to bring them good and repel evil from them—did not suffice them. They availed them nothing when the command of your Lord and His judgment to seize them arrived, or when the punishment of your Lord came.

His saying: وَما زادُوهُمْ غَيْرَ تَتْبِيبٍ **"and they did not increase them in anything but ruin."** Ruin (التتبيب) is destruction and annihilation, derived from *al-tabb* (التب), which originally means cutting off. This is because their worship of idols was a sin necessitating their punishment; when they felt the punishment and misery, they sought refuge in the idols and invoked them to remove it. Their invocation was another sin that increased the intensity of the punishment upon them and the severity of the penalty for them; thus, they increased them in nothing but ruin.

Attributing ruin (التتبيب) to their gods is metaphorical (مجاز); in reality, it is attributed to their act of invoking them, which is an act performed by the invoker, not the one invoked.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَكَذلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذا أَخَذَ الْقُرى وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ **"Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the towns while they are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe" (11:102).**

The reference is to the previously mentioned news of the towns. That is one of the instances (مصاديق) of His seizing by punishment, by which He compared His absolute seizing of towns in its being painful and severe. This is of the nature of universalizing a judgment (التشبيه الكلي) based on some of its instances to indicate that the judgment is general and encompasses all individuals; this is a common type of rhetorical comparison. His saying: إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ **"Indeed His seizing is painful and severe"** is an explanation of the point of resemblance (وجه الشبه), which is pain and severity.

The meaning is: just as Allah, the Glorified, seized these unjust nations—the people of Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, Shu‘ayb, and the people of Pharaoh—with a painful and severe seizing, so too does He seize all other unjust towns when He seizes them. Therefore, let those who take heed learn from this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِمَنْ خافَ عَذابَ الْآخِرَةِ **"There is indeed a sign in that for him who fears the punishment of the Hereafter" (11:103)** to the end of the verse. The reference is to what Allah has related of the stories of those unjust towns which He seized for their injustice with a painful and severe seizing. He informed that His seizing is always thus. In that is a sign (آية) for him who fears the punishment of the life to come, and a mark (علامة) indicating that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, will seize the criminals in the Hereafter for their crimes, and that His seizing will be painful and severe. This necessitates that one takes heed and guards against that which entails the wrath of Allah, the Exalted.

His saying: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ **"That is a day on which mankind shall be gathered" (11:103).** That is, that day in which the punishment of the Hereafter occurs is a day for which mankind shall be gathered. The reference is to the day indicated by the mention of the punishment of the Hereafter, "and for this reason the masculine pronoun was used," as has been said. It is also possible that the masculine gender of the demonstrative pronoun is used to match the predicate (الخبر) with the subject (المبتدأ).

Describing the Last Day as being "gathered for it" (مجموع له) rather than saying "mankind will be gathered" (سيجمع) or "mankind are gathered" (يجمع) is to indicate that the gathering of people for it is one of its necessary attributes (أوصافه المقضية) that is inseparable from it, without needing to be reported as a separate piece of news.

The distinguishing feature of this day is that people are gathered for its sake—the "for" (اللام) denoting purpose (للغاية). Thus, the Day has a status that is not complete except by the gathering of all people, such that not one of them is left behind nor does anyone fail to attend. And the people have a status in which every individual is linked to the whole, where the first is blended with the last and the last with the first, and the whole is mixed with the part and the part with the whole. This status is the accounting of their deeds in terms of faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), obedience (الطاعة) and disobedience (المعصية)—in short, in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة).

For it is clear that a single deed from a single human being draws from all his previous deeds linked to his internal states, and all his subsequent deeds—also linked to his heart's states—draw from it. Likewise, the deed of one individual in relation to the deeds of his fellow humans in terms of influence and being influenced; and likewise, the deeds of the predecessors in relation to the deeds of the successors, and the deeds of the successors in relation to the deeds of the predecessors. Among the predecessors are the leaders of guidance and misguidance who are questioned about the deeds of the successors, and among the successors are the followers and subordinates who are questioned about the deception of their preceding leaders. The Exalted said: فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ **"We will surely question those to whom [Our messengers] were sent, and We will surely question the messengers" (7:6)**, and He said: وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ **"We write down what they have sent ahead and their tracks, and We have counted everything in a manifest leader" (36:12).**

Then the recompense (الجزاء) does not fail the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل).

This status, in this manner, is not complete except by the gathering of all people such that no one is excluded.

From here it becomes clear that the questioning of individuals in their graves and their recompense therein with some reward or punishment—as indicated by the verses of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — and mentioned in detail in the narrations from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام—is distinct from what Allah has informed regarding the accounting of the Day of Resurrection and the recompense decreed there of eternal Paradise or Fire. For what awaits a person in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) is the questioning to complete the record of his deeds to be stored for the Day of Decisive Judgment. The Paradise or Fire in which one resides in the intermediate realm is like a preliminary lodging for the guest prepared for the meeting and the judgment. It is not a complete accounting, nor a final judgment, nor a definitive recompense, as indicated by verses such as: النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ **"The Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in: ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!’" (40:46)**, and His saying: يُسْحَبُونَ فِي الْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ **"they are dragged into the boiling water, then they are kindled in the Fire" (40:71-72).** You see the verse expresses their punishment as being "exposed" to the Fire, then on the Day of Resurrection by "entering" it, which is the severest punishment; and it expresses their punishment by being "dragged" in boiling water, then by being "kindled" in the Fire, which is burning. And the Exalted said: بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ فَرِحِينَ بِما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ **"rather they are alive with their Lord, provided for, rejoicing in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and they rejoice for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve" (3:169-170).** This verse is explicit regarding the world of the grave (عالم القبر) and does not mention an accounting or the eternal Paradise; it only mentions a form of enjoyment in general.

And the Exalted said: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ كَلَّا إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها وَمِنْ وَرائِهِمْ بَرْزَخٌ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ **"When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected" (23:99-100).** The verse mentions that after death they are in a barzakh-life (حياة برزخية) intermediate between the worldly life—which is play and diversion—and the life of the Hereafter, which is the true life, as He said: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ **"The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (itself), had they known" (24:64).**

In summary, the world is the abode of action (دار عمل), the intermediate realm (البرزخ) is the abode of preparation for accounting and recompense, and the Hereafter is the abode of accounting and recompense (دار حساب وجزاء). The Exalted said: يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمانِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا أَتْمِمْ لَنا نُورَنا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا **"the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him. Their light will move in front of them and to their right, and they will say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us’" (66:8).** They attend it with the light they earned in the world and prepared in the intermediate realm (البرزخ), then they ask Him on the Day of Resurrection to perfect their light and remove the remnants of the world of diversion and play.

His saying: وَذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ **"and that is a day witnessed" (11:103)** appears to follow from the previous sentence: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ **"That is a day on which mankind shall be gathered,"** since gathering necessitates witnessing. However, the word is not restricted to mankind; its absolute use (إطلاقه) suggests it is witnessed by everyone capable of witnessing, such as humans, angels, and jinn. The numerous verses indicating the gathering of jinn and devils and the presence of angels there support the absolute sense of "witnessed" as mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَما نُؤَخِّرُهُ إِلَّا لِأَجَلٍ مَعْدُودٍ **"And We do not delay it except for a numbered term" (11:104).** That is, for that day there is a term (أجل) decreed by Allah that it shall not occur before its arrival. Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment nor to repel His decree. He does not delay the Day except for a term that He counts; when the count is complete and the term arrives, the word is fulfilled and the Day occurs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave" (11:105).** The subject of "comes" (يأتي) is a pronoun referring to the previously mentioned "term" (الأجل). That is, the day the term arrives—to which the Resurrection is delayed—no soul shall speak except by His leave. The Exalted said: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ **"Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come" (29:5).**

Some have mentioned, as in *al-Majma‘*, that the meaning is: the day the Resurrection and recompense come. This necessitates referring the pronoun to the Resurrection and recompense because the preceding speech points to them in a way; however, this is an unnecessary affectation (تكلف).

Others have mentioned—as in the *Tafsir* of the author of *al-Manar*—that the meaning is: at the time when that specific day arrives, no speaking soul shall speak except by the leave of Allah, the Exalted. Thus, "day" (يوم) in the verse refers to absolute time (مطلق الوقت)—i.e., the undefined—because it is the vessel for the defined day described by what was mentioned, which is the subject of "comes."

This is an error, as it necessitates the day being a vessel for itself (ظرفية اليوم لليوم), for the meaning would essentially return to our saying: "in the time when that specific time arrives" or "the day when that specific day arrives." Distinguishing between the two "days" by making one specific and defined and the other general and undefined does not help in repelling the impossibility of a thing being its own vessel, or time—which is a vessel by its nature—being a vessel for another time. This is an impossibility that does not become possible by changing the wording.

What he mentioned regarding the distinction between the two days by being absolute or defined is mere conceptualization that avails nothing. For the day in which that described day arrives and that described day itself are equal in terms of being absolute, defined, breadth, and narrowness. Yes, sometimes time is taken as identical to the events occurring within it, thus becoming one of the events and its vessel-nature is ignored, making it a content (مظروف) for another time, as when it is said: "the Day of Sacrifice is in the month of Dhu al-Hijjah" or "the Day of ‘Ashura is in Muharram." The Exalted said: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ **"And on the day when the Hour sets in" (45:27).** If this consideration is valid in the verse, it would be possible for the pronoun of "comes" to refer to the Day.

His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"no soul shall speak except by His leave."** That is, no soul among those present shall speak except by the leave of Allah, the Glorified. The deletion of one of the two *ta's* (تاءين) that meet in the future tense of the *tafa‘‘ul* (تفعل) form is common and regular.

The preposition "by" (بـ) in His saying: بِإِذْنِهِ **"by His leave"** denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة). Thus, the exception is in reality from the speech, not from the speaker, as in His saying: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ **"they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (78:38).** The meaning is: no soul shall speak any speech except with the speech that accompanies His leave—unlike the world, where one speaks what he chooses and intends, whether Allah has permitted it by legislative leave (إذن تشريع) or not.

This attribute—namely, the lack of any soul speaking except by His leave—has been mentioned as one of the defining characteristics of the Day of Resurrection. However, it is not exclusive to it, for no soul ever speaks, nor does any event ever occur, except by His leave, without this being exclusive to the Day of Resurrection.

It has been previously mentioned in some of our earlier discussions that most of what is mentioned in the Noble Quran as defining characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, in the context of attributes specific to it, actually encompasses it and other times. For example, His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ **"the day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" (40:16)**, and His saying: يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ **"the day when you will turn back to flee, with no one to protect you from Allah" (40:33)**, and His saying: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمئِذٍ لِلَّهِ **"the day when no soul will be able to do anything for another soul, and the command that day will belong to Allah" (82:19)**, among other verses. It is well known that nothing is ever hidden from Him, and nothing ever has a protector from Him, and no soul ever possesses anything for another soul except by His leave, and to Him belong creation and command always.

However, that which contemplation (التدبر) leads to in verses such as: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ **"You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22)**, and His saying relating the words of the criminals: رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ **"Our Lord! We have seen and heard. So send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we [now] have certainty" (32:12)**, and His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ **"On the day We will muster them all together, then We will say to the polytheists, ‘Stay where you are, you and your partners!’ Then We will separate them from each other"**—until He says—هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ **"There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their true Master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them" (10:28-30)**—is that the Day of Resurrection is a vessel in which Allah gathers the servants and removes the cover and veil from them. Thus, realities appear therein with complete clarity, and that which is behind the veil of the Unseen (الغيب) in this life is revealed. At that point, no doubt or suspicion will stir in their breasts, nor will any thought occur to their hearts; they will witness that Allah is the Manifest Truth (الحق المبين), and they will see that all power belongs to Allah, and that sovereignty, protection, command, and subjugation belong to Him alone, without any partner.

Causes (الأسباب) will fall away from the independence that was imagined for them in the life of the world; the connection will be severed, and the links of influence between things will vanish. At that point, the stars of causes will be scattered, and the stars by which delusions were guided in their darkness will be obliterated. No possessor of sovereignty will have a sovereignty by which he is independent, nor will any possessor of power have that with which he is emboldened. Nothing will have a refuge or sanctuary to flee to, nor a protection to be guarded by, nor a veil to hide anything from another. The command will be entirely for Allah, the One, the All-paramount; none possesses anything but He (1).

This is the meaning of His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ **"the day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah"**, and His saying: ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ **"with no one to protect you from Allah"**, and His saying: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمئِذٍ لِلَّهِ **"the day when no soul will be able to do anything for another soul, and the command that day will belong to Allah"**, and other such verses. They all negate what the delusions of people decorate in this worldly life—which is nothing but diversion and play—namely, that these causes possess the meaning of influence, and are clothed with the attributes of sovereignty, power, protection, might, and honor in a real, independent manner, and that they are the givers, withholders, benefactors, and harmers, with no goal beyond them and no good besides them.

From here, one can find familiarity with the meaning of His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave."** This meaning has been repeated in other verses with similar wording, such as: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً **"they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right" (78:38)**, and His saying: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak" (77:35).**

This is because Allah, the Exalted, says in describing this day: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ **"the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9)**, and He says: إِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ **"Whether you reveal what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284).** Thus, He explains that the accounting on that day is based on what is in the souls of states and accidents (الأحوال والأعراض), whether good or evil, not by what is discovered from them through the means of discovery as in this worldly life.

So, that which was under the veils of secrecy in the world—the hidden things of the souls and the folded contents of the hearts—is manifest and uncovered on the Day of Resurrection. What is "unseen" (غيب) today is "witnessed" (شهادة) tomorrow. The speaking (التكلم) that we humans practice among ourselves is only through the use of composed sounds that indicate, by way of convention and agreement (الوضع والاعتبار), meanings that reside in our inner selves. The motive for us to establish and practice it is the social need for some of us to be guided to what is in the minds of others, due to the impossibility of the senses perceiving it.

Speaking is one of the social causes (الأسباب الاجتماعية) we use to reveal the hidden meanings in the mind; it is predicated on what is in the minds being outside the direct perception of a human being. If we were aided by a sense that could reach mental meanings and witness them—just as, for example, sight reaches light and colors, and touch reaches heat, cold, roughness, and smoothness—we would not need to establish languages and speak them, nor would there be among us what is called a "word" or "speech." Likewise, if the human species lived its worldly life in an individualistic rather than social manner, there would be no news of speech, nor would its seed have been formed.

All of this is because the worldly life is composed of the witnessed (شهادة) and the unseen (غيب)—that which is sensed and witnessed, and that which is beyond the senses. People are in dire need of revealing and knowing the intentions in their minds. If a realm of life were assumed to be purely witnessed, composed of witnessed matters, there would be nothing in it necessitating speaking and speech. If we were to apply the term "speech" to any of the states existing there, its instance (مصداق) would be the appearance of some of what is in the souls of people to others and the awareness of those others of it.

This realm described as such is the realm of Resurrection (نشأة القيامة), according to what Allah, the Glorified, describes in verses like: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ **"the day when the secrets are examined."** This is what appears from His saying: لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌّ **"On that day neither man nor jinn will be questioned about his sin"**—until He says—يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ **"The guilty will be recognized by their marks; so they will be seized by their forelocks and their feet" (55:39-41).**

If you say: "Based on this, there is no meaning for the occurrence of lying and falsehood there, yet the Noble Quran has explicitly mentioned it," as in His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا أَيْنَ شُرَكاؤُكُمُ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ **"On the day We muster them all together, We will say to those who ascribed partners [to Allah], ‘Where are your partners whom you used to claim?’ Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ See how they lie against themselves" (6:22-24)**, and His saying: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلى شَيْءٍ أَلا إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ **"The day Allah resurrects them all together, they will swear to Him as they swear to you, and they will think that they are on some [solid] footing. Look! They are indeed the liars" (58:18).**

I say: This is from the manifestation of ingrained dispositions (الملكات). Just as a human being, when thinking, witnesses the hidden parts of his soul without needing to inform himself of what he is thinking or reveal what is in his mind to himself through speaking—because he is in a state of witnessing (شهادة) his inner self, not in the unseen (غيب)—he nevertheless conceives an image of speech that indicates the mental meanings he is observing. He may even speak with his tongue what occurs to his mind of the parts of the thought, and the motive for this is what he has become accustomed to of speaking and uttering when he expresses what is in his mind to others.

These polytheists and hypocrites, having become accustomed to lying in their worldly life and having lived on the lies of delusion, this manifests from them on the day when dispositions and psychological habits manifest. Otherwise, it is impossible for a human being to stand before his Lord—while He, the Exalted, witnesses his inner and outer self, his deeds are present, his record is spread open, the witnesses are standing, his limbs are speaking of what they did, and causes (including lying) have fallen away and perished—and then lie in the hope of deceiving Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, prevailing over Him with a deceptive, false argument, and being saved thereby.

This is similar to their being called on the Day of Resurrection to prostrate and then being unable to do so. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ **"The day the catastrophe occurs and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, they will not be able [to do it]. Their looks will be humbled, and abasement will overcome them. They were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound" (68:42-43).** Their inability to prostrate is only due to the rootedness of the disposition of arrogance (ملكة الاستكبار) in their souls. If it were due to a new prevention from His side, the Exalted, they would have an argument against Him.

If you say: "If it were as you mentioned, and there were no need for speaking there nor any instance for it, then what is the meaning of the exception in His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"no soul shall speak except by His leave"** and similar verses? And what is the meaning of what is repeated in various places of His speech relating their sayings?"

I say: There is no doubt that a human being, while in this life, is an agent with choice (مختار) in his actions, among which is speaking. He has an equal relation to every one of his acts and its omission; both are equal in relation to him. When he commits the act, for example, one of the two sides becomes determined by necessity (تعيناً اضطرارياً), and there is no news of choice after that. The necessary effects that follow the act—among which is the recompense earned by the act—have the same status as the act after its realization.

The realm of the Hereafter is the abode of recompense (دار جزاء), not the abode of action (دار عمل). Thus, there is no news of human choice there; there is only the human being and his deed which he brought and which has become necessarily inseparable from him, and that which the deed is linked to of records and witnesses, and his Lord to Whom the matter returns and in Whose hand is the Decisive Judgment. When he is called, he responds by necessity, as the Exalted said: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ الدَّاعِيَ لا عِوَجَ لَهُ **"On that day they will follow the caller in whom there is no crookedness" (20:108)**, whereas they used to be called to the truth in the world and would not respond. When he speaks in response to a question, it is not of the same nature as worldly speaking which originated from his choice and revealed something hidden in his soul. For his mouth has been sealed and he has no way to speak what he wants, how he wants. The Exalted said: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ **"Today We seal their mouths, and their hands speak to Us, and their feet bear witness to what they used to earn" (36:65).** And He said: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses" (77:35-36).** For an excuse (العذر) only exists in a recompense where there is a trace of choice, and for whose realization there is the possibility of existence or non-existence. As for the evil deed that is finished and the recompense that follows it by necessity, there is no room for an excuse therein. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ **"O you who disbelieve! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7).** That is, your recompense is the very deed you performed; it does not change by an excuse or a pretext. It would only change if it were a worldly recompense whose matter was in the hand of the judging requiter who chooses what he sees and wills.

In summary: if he speaks in response to a question, his speaking is out of necessity and corresponds to the manifest deed he has, which has no cover over it there at all. If he speaks a lie, it is of the nature of the manifestation of dispositions, as previously mentioned, and one of his deeds that manifests as a manifestation, not as speech considered an answer to a question. Thus, his mouth is sealed, and his hearing, sight, skin, hand, and foot are made to speak; the deed he performed is brought forth, the witnesses are summoned, and Allah is a witness over all things.

It has been summarized from all we have presented that the meaning of His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"no soul shall speak except by His leave"** is that speaking on that day is not in the manner of worldly speaking—as an intentional revelation of what is in the mind such that the speaker can be truthful or lie. For this intentional possession (التملك الاختياري), which is among the concomitants of the abode of action, is removed there. Thus, the human being has no choice in his speaking; rather, it is contingent upon the leave and will of Allah. If you contemplate well, you will find that the outcome of this view—namely, the removal of the rule of choice from human speaking and all other actions, and the encompassing of the meaning of necessity (الاضطرار) over everything on that day—returns to what we opened the discussion with: that the characteristic of this day is the uncovering of the realities of things therein and the return of the Unseen (الغيب) to the Witnessed (شهادة). You must firmly contemplate the knowledge that the Divine Speech imparts regarding the Return (المعاد), for it is a difficult, intricate, and deep matter.

Some have mentioned that the meaning of His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"no soul shall speak except by His leave"** is that they do not speak therein except with good speech permitted by the Law (شرعاً), because people are compelled there to abandon foul things, so no foul thing occurs from them; as for what is not foul, it is permitted.

In this is the objection that it is a specification without a specifier (تخصيص من غير مخصص). The Day is not a day of action such that leave is given for performing a good act and not for a foul one. The compulsion (الإلجاء) whose source is that the realm is one of recompense, not action, does not distinguish between a good and a foul act, given that both are intentional, because "good" and "foul" are only applied to intentional acts.

Furthermore, Allah, the Exalted, says: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses."** It is well known that offering excuses is not a foul act in any way.

Others have said: The meaning of the verse is that no one speaks in the Hereafter with beneficial speech—such as intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — or a means—except by His leave.

This reduces the verse in terms of its indication to verses like: يَوْمَئِذٍ لا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ **"Intercession will not avail that day except from him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (20:109).** In this is the objection that it is a restriction without evidence. If that were intended, the speech would properly have been: "no soul shall speak for a soul or regarding a soul except by His leave," as occurred in its counterpart: لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً **"no soul will be able to do anything for another soul."**

From what we have presented, the way to reconcile the verses affirming speaking on the Day of Resurrection and the verses negating it has been obtained.

To clarify: the verses dealing with the issue of speaking therein are of two types: one type negates speaking or affirms it for individuals without exception, such as: لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌّ **"neither man nor jinn will be questioned about his sin" (55:39)**, and His saying: يَوْمَ تَأْتِي كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها **"The day when every soul will come pleading for itself" (16:111).**

The other type negates speech of any kind, whether truthful or false, such as: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak" (77:35)**, and His saying: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَلا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ **"Now we have no intercessors, nor any sympathetic friend" (26:100-101).**

The first type is reconciled between its two sides by verses like: لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ **"they do not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted" (78:38).** The second type has the contradiction between its sides removed by the verse under discussion: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave"**—but based on what was previously clarified regarding the meaning of speaking being contingent upon His leave, such that it implies they are compelled in what they speak, forced into what Allah, the Glorified, permits. They do not have the right to speak what they choose and intend as they did in the world, so that this becomes one of the attributes specific to the Day of Resurrection.

By this, the deficiency in what the author of *al-Manar* mentioned in his *Tafsir* becomes apparent, where he said in explaining the verse: "And the negation of speech on that day except by His leave, the Exalted, explains for us the reconciliation between the verses negating it absolutely and those affirming it absolutely." End quote. He had mentioned before it verses including: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak"** and الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ **"Today We seal their mouths"** the verse.

This is because—firstly—he did not distinguish between the two types of verses, which gave the impression that the negation of speech except by His leave in the verse under discussion is sufficient to remove the contradiction between the verses absolutely, which is not the case.

And—secondly—he did not explain the meaning of speech being by His leave, the Exalted, so the objection of specifying the Day of Resurrection in the verse with something that is not exclusive to it was directed at him.

The objection of contradiction may be answered in another way: that the Day of Resurrection comprises various stations (مواقف); they are permitted to speak in some of those stations and not permitted in others. This has been mentioned in some narrations.

This answer, although outwardly distinct from the previous way, is not independent of the issue of leave; it in reality returns to it.

It may also be answered that the meaning of the lack of speaking and utterance is that they do not utter an argument (حجة); they only speak by confessing their sins, or some of them to others, and casting sins upon one another. This is like when someone says to one who speaks much but without an argument: "You have said nothing and uttered nothing." Thus, the one who speaks without an argument is called "not speaking" because he did not bring the truth of speech which ought to contain an argument; it is as if it is not speech. So the negation of speaking looks at considering speech that is of no avail as non-speech by way of claim (ادعاء).

In this is the objection: if it were valid, it would only be valid in verses like: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak."** As for verses like: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave"**, it does not return to a coherent meaning.

It may also be answered—as al-Alusi narrated from *al-Ghurar wa al-Durar* by al-Murtada—that the Day of Resurrection is a long, extended day, so it is possible that they are prevented from utterance in part of it and permitted in another part of it.

In this is the objection that the reference is to the Day of Resurrection in its length. According to their view, the meaning of His saying: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ **"This is a day they will not speak"** would be: "this is a day they will not speak in part of it," which is contrary to the apparent meaning (خلاف الظاهر). A similar objection applies to the second way in which the contradiction was answered by the difference of stations, for the essence of the two ways—the second and this fourth—is one. The only difference is that the second way removes the contradiction by the difference of places, and this way removes it by the difference of times, just as the third way removes it by the difference of speech in its containing utility or lacking it.

It may also be answered by what appears from the saying of some: that the exception in His saying: لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ **"no soul shall speak except by His leave"** is disconnected (منقطع), not connected (متصل). That is: no soul shall speak by a power from itself, but [it speaks] by the leave of Allah, the Exalted. The gist of this way is that the forbidden speaking on the Day of Resurrection is that which is by the power of the human being, and the permissible, occurring speaking is what is by the leave of Allah, the Exalted.

In this is the objection: the speaking of a human being, like all his other intentional actions, is never attributed to his power purely at any time; rather, it is attributed to his power as derived from the power of Allah and His leave. Whenever a human being speaks or performs an act by his power, it proceeds from his power accompanied by the leave of Allah. The meaning of the exception then returns to the cancellation of all causes operating in speaking on the Day of Resurrection except one of them, which is His leave, the Exalted. The exception becomes connected (متصل) and returns to the first way we presented: that the forbidden speaking is the intentional kind on the model of worldly speaking, and the permissible is what was attributed to the Divine cause only, which is His leave and will. The realm is one of necessity (الاضطرار) and compulsion (الإلجاء). However, they see that the cause of compulsion on the Day of Resurrection is the witnessing of its terrors, for people are compelled upon witnessing terrors to confess, acknowledge, speak the truth, and follow the truth. We have already presented that the cause for this is that the realm is one of recompense, not action, and the manifestation of realities at that point.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ **"Among them some are wretched and some felicitous" (11:105).** Felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) are opposites. The felicity of everything is to attain the good (الخير) belonging to its existence by which it becomes perfect and in which it finds pleasure. In the human being—who is composed of spirit and body—it is to attain good according to his bodily and spiritual faculties, thus enjoying it and finding pleasure. His wretchedness is to lose that and be deprived of it. Thus, according to technical terminology, they are of the nature of privation and possession (العدم والملكة). The difference between felicity and good is that felicity is the good specific to the species or the individual, while good is more general.

The apparent sense of His saying: فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ **"Among them some are wretched and some felicitous"** does not imply the restriction of the gathered people to these two groups.

This is outwardly consistent with His division of people into believer (مؤمن), disbeliever (كافر), and the oppressed (مستضعف)—such as children, the insane, and all those for whom the argument was not completed in the world. However, the purpose for which the verses are related is not to explain the categories of people according to action and merit, but rather in terms of the status of this day: that it is a day for which mankind is gathered and a witnessed day from which no one is absent, and that it ends in Paradise or Fire.

The oppressed, although they are a third category in relation to those who merit Paradise by their deeds and those who merit the Fire by their deeds, it is necessary that they do not go to waste, nor does the state of ambiguity and waiting continue for them. They are ultimately joined to one of the two groups: the felicitous or the wretched, entering into what they enter of Paradise or Fire. The Exalted said: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ **"And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He will punish them, or He will turn to them clemently, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise" (9:106).** This context necessitates that the gathered people be restricted to the two groups: the felicitous and the wretched; there is none among them but is felicitous or wretched.

The verse is similar to His saying in another place: وَتُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ الْجَمْعِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَلكِنْ يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمُونَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ **"and warn [them] of the Day of Assembly, in which there is no doubt: a part will be in paradise and a part in the Blaze. Had Allah willed, He would have made them one community; but He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and the wrongdoers have no guardian or helper" (42:7-8).** Here, the sentence "a part will be in paradise and a part in the Blaze" (فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ), with the aid of the context, implies restriction, even if on its own it lacks such indication.

That which the verse indicates is that whoever is there among the gathered people is either wretched, characterized by wretchedness, or felicitous, clothed in felicity. As for how these two attributes were established for their subjects—and whether they are essential (ذاتيان) to those characterized by them, or established for them by an eternal will whose object never fails it, or established for them through acquisition and action while the subjects were devoid of them in terms of their essence—the verse does not look at any of that. However, the occurrence of the verse in the context of the call to faith and righteous deeds, and the exhortation to choose obedience and abandon disobedience, indicates the facilitation of the path to attaining felicity, as He said: ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ **"then He made the way easy for him" (80:20).**

By this, the invalidity of what some have inferred from the verse becomes apparent—namely, the necessity of felicity and wretchedness for the human being based on His judgment therein. Al-Razi said in his *Tafsir* under this verse: "Know that He has now judged some of the people of Resurrection to be felicitous and some to be wretched. Whoever Allah has judged with a judgment and known that matter, it is impossible for it to be otherwise. Otherwise, it would necessitate that the report of Allah becomes a lie and His knowledge becomes ignorance, and that is impossible. Thus, it is established that the felicitous does not turn wretched, and the wretched does not turn felicitous."

He said: "It is narrated from ‘Umar that he said: 'When His saying: فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ **"Among them some are wretched and some felicitous"** was revealed, I said: O Messenger of Allah, upon what then do we act? Upon something that has been finished, or upon something that has not been finished? He replied: Upon something that has been finished, O ‘Umar, and with which the pens have dried and the decrees have run—but everyone is facilitated for that for which he was created.'" He said: "The Mu‘tazilah said: It is narrated from al-Hasan that he said: 'Among them some are wretched by their deeds and some are felicitous by their deeds.' We say: the definitive proof is not repelled by these narrations."

"Also, there is no dispute that one only becomes wretched by his deed and felicitous by his deed; but since that deed occurred by the decree (قضاء) and measure (قدر) of Allah, the proof we mentioned remains." End quote.

This is a strange sophistry (مغالطة). As for what he called a "definitive proof," he committed a sophistry therein by taking the time of the judgment as the time for its result and effect. It is self-evident that a true judgment now regarding the characterization of a subject with an attribute in the future does not necessitate the characterization except in the future, not in the time of the judgment subsisting in the judge, which is now. Just as our judgment at night that the air will be illuminated after a few hours—which is a true judgment—does not necessitate the illumination of the air at night. And our judgment that the child will become a senile old man after eighty years does not require him being a senile old man at the time of the judgment.

So His saying: فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ **"Among them some are wretched and some felicitous"**—which is a report from Him, the Exalted, that a group of them are wretched on the Day of Resurrection and others are felicitous on the Day of Resurrection—if it were a judgment of their wretchedness and felicity as such, it is only a judgment proceeding from Him in this "now" that they are such-and-such on the Day of Resurrection. It is accepted that it does not change from what it is in its proper time; otherwise, it would necessitate that His report be a lie and His knowledge be ignorance. It is not a judgment proceeding from Him in this "now" that they are such-and-such in this "now," nor is it a judgment proceeding from Him in this "now" that they are such-and-such always. This is manifest.

I wonder what prevented him from judging with such a judgment regarding all other attributes of people on the Day of Resurrection that Allah has informed of—thus judging that they are believers always, or disbelievers always, and in Paradise before the Day of Resurrection, and in the Fire before the Day of Resurrection—since his proof applies to them and others just as it applies to wretchedness and felicity.

As for the narration he cited, containing the saying of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "but everyone is facilitated for that for which he was created," it has no indication for what he mentioned at all, and the clarification of that will come in the upcoming discussion of narrations, if Allah wills.

As for his final statement: "there is no dispute that one only becomes wretched by his deed and felicitous by his deed; but since that deed occurred by the decree and measure of Allah, the proof we mentioned remains"—he means that the attachment of the decree (القضاء) to the deed—and it is impossible for its object to fail that which was decreed for it—necessitates its becoming of necessary occurrence. Thus, the act becomes compelled (مجبر عليه), not intentional (اختياري) with the act and omission being equal in relation to the agent. The agent has no influence in it, nor does the deed have any influence in the attainment of wretchedness or felicity. Rather, between the agent and his act, and between the act and the effect occurring after it of wretchedness or felicity, there is only a coincidental accompaniment (صحابة اتفاقية) by which the custom of Allah, the Glorified, has run to bring this into existence before that, and that after this, without any real link between the two matters nor any real influence of one in the other.

This is another sophistry arising from the confusion between the relation of necessity (الوجوب) and the relation of possibility (الإمكان). For the deed has a complete cause (علة تامة) by which its existence becomes necessary, which is the will of the human being, the soundness of his tools of action, the existence of a matter receptive to the action, time, place, and the absence of obstacles and impediments, among others. When these gather, are complete, and perfected, the occurrence of the deed becomes necessary. Thus, the deed has a relation to them which is the relation of necessity; and it has a relation to each one of the parts of its complete cause—among which is the will of the human being—which is the relation of possibility. For the deed's existence does not become necessary merely by the realization of the will alone; rather, it is possible, and only becomes necessary if the remaining parts of the cause are joined to it.

So the deed realized by the necessity of the complete cause, in this very state, has a relation of necessity to the sum of the complete cause, and a relation of possibility to the will of the human being. Its necessary relation to the complete cause does not nullify its possible relation to the will of the human being, nor does it turn it from possibility to necessity. Rather, the relation of the deed to the human being is always by possibility, just as its relation to the sum resulting from the human being and the rest of the parts of the complete cause is always by necessity. The two sides of action and omission are always equal in relation to the human being, just as one of the two sides of action and omission is always determined in view of the complete cause.

The result is that the act is intentional (اختياري) for the human being while simultaneously not being devoid of a complete cause necessitating its existence. The definitive decree (القضاء الحتم) is among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), derived from the station of action, which is the chain of causes ordered according to the system of existence. The fact that effects are necessary in relation to their causes—i.e., the necessity of every decreed thing (مقضي) in relation to that which the Divine decree attached to it—does not contradict its being intentional for the human being, with its relation to him being one of possibility. Thus, it has become clear that he took the relation of the deed to the human being as a relation of necessity, not possibility, by the delusion that the deed's being of necessary occurrence by the Divine decree necessitates its being of necessary occurrence in relation to the human being, not possible.

In another clear explanation: the attachment of His knowledge, the Exalted, for example, that such-and-such a piece of wood will be burned by fire, necessitates the necessity of the realization of the burning *as conditioned by the fire*, because that is what the true knowledge attached to—not the necessity of the realization of the burning absolutely, whether there was fire or not, since no knowledge was realized with this attribute. Likewise, His knowledge, the Exalted, that the human being will act by his choice and will a certain deed, or that he will become wretched due to such-and-such an intentional deed, necessitates the necessity of the realization of the deed *by way of the human being's choice*—not the necessity of the realization of such-and-such a deed whether there was choice or not, and whether there was a human being or not, such that the link of influence between the human being and his deed is severed. Similar to this is His knowledge that such-and-such a human being will become wretched by his disbelief intentionally; it necessitates the realization of the wretchedness *that arises from disbelief*, not wretchedness absolutely, whether there was disbelief or not.

Thus, it is clear that His knowledge, the Exalted, of the human being's deed does not necessitate the nullification of choice and the establishment of compulsion, even though the object of His knowledge does not fail His knowledge. To Him belongs the judgment; there is none to reverse His judgment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا فَفِي النَّارِ لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ **"As for the wretched, they shall be in the Fire: their lot therein will be groaning and wailing" (11:106).** It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "The groaning (الزفير) is the beginning of the braying of a donkey, and the wailing (الشهيق) is the end of its braying." End quote. It is said in *al-Kashshaf*: "The groaning (الزفير) is the exhaling of breath, and the wailing (الشهيق) is its inhaling." End quote. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "**The groaning (الزفير)** is the vibration of breath until the ribs swell from it." And he said: "The wailing (الشهيق) is the length of the groaning, and it is its inhaling, while the groaning is its exhaling." The Exalted said: لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ **"their lot therein will be groaning and wailing"**, and سَمِعُوا لَها تَغَيُّظاً وَزَفِيراً **"they will hear its raging and groaning" (25:12)**, and the Exalted said: سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً **"they will hear its wailing" (67:7)**. Its root is from a "lofty mountain" (جبل شاهق), i.e., of extreme height. End quote.

The meanings—as you see—are close, and there is a metaphor (استعارة) in the speech. The meaning is that they draw their breaths into their chests and then expel them, prolonging them by raising their voices in weeping and moaning due to the intensity of the Fire's heat and the greatness of the distress and calamity, just as a donkey does that when braying.

It was apparent from the context of His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" (So some of them are wretched and some are happy) that it should be followed by: "As for those who are wretched, they shall be in the Fire, for them therein is moaning and sighing, etc." However, the preceding context with which the description of the Day of Resurrection opened—namely His saying: "ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ" (That is a day on which mankind will be gathered, and that is a day witnessed)—is built upon the notion of multiplicity and the collective (الجماعة). The requirement of this is to proceed in the plural form: "those who are wretched" and "those who are happy."

He only expressed it with the words "wretched" (شقي) and "happy" (سعيد) because of what was said before it: "لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ" (no soul shall speak). Thus, the indefinite singular (المفرد المنكر) was chosen to signify negation with total exhaustiveness (الاستغراق) and universality (العموم). Once the purpose was achieved by His saying: "لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" (no soul shall speak except by His leave. So some of them are wretched and some are happy), the previous context based on multiplicity and the collective returned, and it was said: "فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا" (As for those who are wretched) in the plural form, until the end of the three verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ** — *They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish. Indeed your Lord does whatever He desires.* (11:107). This is an exposition of the stay of the people of the Fire therein, just as the following verse: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** — *As for the happy, they will be in paradise. They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty.* (11:108) is an exposition of the stay of the people of Paradise therein and a confirmation of their stability in their abode.

Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Immortality (الخلود) is the freedom of a thing from the onset of corruption (الفساد) and its remaining in the state it is in. Everything for which change and corruption are delayed is described by the Arabs as 'immortal' (الخلود), such as their saying for the tripod stones (الأثافي) — *the stones upon which a cooking pot is placed*: 'immortals' (خوالد), and that is due to the length of their stay, not the eternity of their existence. It is said: 'he remained' (خلد), 'he remains' (يخلد), 'immortality' (خلودا). The Exalted said: 'لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ' (that you may live forever). And *al-khuld* (الخلد) — *with a fatha then a sukun* — is a name for the part that remains of a human being in its state, so it does not transform as long as the human is alive like the transformation of the rest of his parts. The root of *al-mukhallad* (المخلد) is that which remains for a long period; from this, it is said: 'a man made immortal' (رجل مخلد) for one whose gray hair is delayed, and 'an immortal beast' (دابة مخلدة) is one whose central incisors remain until its lateral incisors emerge. Then it was used metaphorically for that which remains eternally."

"Immortality (الخلود) in Paradise is the remaining of things in the state they are in without the onset of corruption upon them. The Exalted said: 'أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ' (they shall be the inhabitants of paradise; they shall remain in it [forever]), 'أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ' (they shall be the inhabitants of the Fire; they shall remain in it [forever]), and 'وَمَنْ يقتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ خالِداً فِيها' (Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to remain in it [forever])."

"And His saying, the Exalted: 'يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ' (They will be waited upon by immortal youths). It is said: they are kept in their state and corruption does not affect them. It is also said: they are adorned with earrings (مقرطون) called *khulada* (بخلدة), and *al-khulda* (الخلدة) is a type of earring. And **making immortal** (إخلاد) a thing is to make it remain or to judge it as remaining. Based on this is His saying, the Glorified: 'وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ' (but he clung to the earth), meaning he leaned toward it, thinking he would remain immortal therein." End of quote.

His saying: "ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ" (as long as the heavens and the earth endure) is a type of restriction (التقييد) that serves to emphasize immortality (الخلود). The meaning is: remaining therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure. However, the Quranic verses are explicit that the heavens and the earth do not endure for eternity (دوام الأبد), while they are also explicit regarding the permanence of Paradise and the Fire—a permanence that does not lead to annihilation or cessation.

Among the verses explicit on the first point is His saying, the Exalted: **ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — *We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a stated term.* (46:3); and His saying: **يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ** — *The day We shall roll up the sky like the rolling of the scrolls for writings. We will bring it back as We began the first creation—a promise [binding] on Us. Indeed We will do it.* (21:104); and His saying: **وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ** — *and the heavens will be rolled up in His right hand.* (39:67); and His saying: **إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا** — *when the earth is shaken with a [violent] shaking, and the mountains are shattered into [countless] fragments, and become scattered dust.* (56:4-6).

Among those explicit on the second point is His saying, the Exalted: **جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً** — *gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever.* (64:9); and His saying: **وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيراً خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً لا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً** — *and He has prepared for them a blaze, to remain in it forever; they will not find any guardian or helper.* (33:64-65).

Accordingly, a problem arises in the two verses from two aspects:

First: Defining eternal immortality (الخلود المؤبد) by the duration of the heavens and the earth, while they are not eternal according to the aforementioned verses.

Second: Defining the immortal matter that begins from the Day of Resurrection—which is the state of the two groups in Paradise and the Fire and their stability therein—by that whose existence ends at the Day of Resurrection, namely the heavens and the earth. This second problem is more difficult than the first, because it applies even to those who do not believe in immortality in the Fire, or in both Paradise and the Fire, unlike the first.

What resolves the problem is that He, the Exalted, mentions in His speech that in the Hereafter there are an earth and heavens, even if they are different from those in the world in some way. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — *The day the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount.* (14:48). And He said, narrating from the people of Paradise: **وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ** — *They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us heirs to the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’* (39:74). And He said, promising the believers and describing them: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ** — *For such the ultimate abode will be.* (13:22).

Thus, for the Hereafter, there are heavens and an earth, just as there are Paradise and the Fire, and for both, there are inhabitants. Allah, the Glorified, has described all of them as being "with Him," and He said: **ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** — *What is with you will be exhausted, and what is with Allah will remain.* (16:96). Thus, He judged that they are permanent (باقية) and not subject to annihilation (فانية).

The definition of the permanence of Paradise and the Fire and their inhabitants by the duration of the heavens and the earth is only because the heavens and the earth, in an absolute sense and insofar as they are heavens and earth, are eternal and not subject to annihilation. It is only these heavens and this earth in this world, according to the observed system, that will perish. As for the heavens that shade Paradise, for example, and the earth that carries it—having shone with the light of its Lord—they are firm and non-vanishing. Thus, the universe is never devoid of them. By this, both problems are repelled.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* pointed to this view in summary when he said: "The evidence that it has heavens and an earth is His saying, the Glorified: 'يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ' and His saying, the Glorified: 'وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ'. And because the earth of the Hereafter must have that which carries them and shades them—either a heaven that Allah creates or the Throne shades them, and everything that shades you is a heaven (سماء)." End of quote.

However, the second aspect he pointed to is weak (سخيف), because it establishes the heaven and the earth from the perspective of relation (الإضافة)—that Paradise and the Fire must be conceived as having an 'above' and a 'below.' Thus, Paradise and the Fire would be the primary entities (أصلا), and their heaven and earth would be subordinate (تبعين) to them in existence. The consequence of this would be defining the permanence of their heaven and earth by the duration of Paradise and the Fire, not the reverse as was done in the verse.

Furthermore, the consequence of this view is that Paradise and the Fire would have an earth and a heaven (singular), but not "heavens" (السماوات) in the plural as in the verse. Thus, the problem regarding the "heavens" would remain as it is.

By what has preceded, the objection raised by al-Qadi in his *Tafsir* is also repelled, where he said: "There is a point of contention here, for it is a comparison to something whose existence and permanence most of creation do not know. And whoever knows it only knows it through what indicates the permanence of reward and punishment, so the comparison is of no benefit to him." End of quote.

His meaning is that the verse compares the permanence of Paradise and the Fire with their inhabitants to the permanence of the heavens and the earth. If the intended meaning were the heavens and earth of the Hereafter, and most of creation do not know their existence or permanence, then this would be a comparison of the more manifest (الأجلى) to the more hidden (الأخفى), which is not permissible in eloquent speech.

The response to him is: We only know the permanence of Paradise and the Fire with their inhabitants from His speech, the Exalted, just as we know the existence of heavens and an earth for them, as well as the eternity of all of them from His speech. So what prevents defining one of two realities disclosed by His speech—in terms of permanence—by the other in His speech? This is so even if one of the two realities is better known to people than the other, since both are taken from His speech and not from outside of it.

By this, what al-Alusi mentioned at the end of this discussion is also repelled: that what is immediately understood (المتبادر) from the heavens and the earth are these familiar celestial bodies; thus, it is better to seek another explanation there besides this one. End of summary.

The reason for this repulsion is that the Quranic verses only follow the understanding of the people of the language in their universal concepts (المفاهيم الكلية) provided by language and custom. However, in their objectives and the identification of the instances (المصاديق) to which the concepts apply, they do not. Rather, the path followed therein is contemplation (التدبر) which Allah, the Glorified, commanded, and returning the ambiguous (المتشابه) to the clear (المحكم), and comparing verse to verse. For the Quran bears witness to itself and speaks through itself, and parts of it confirm others—as stated in the narrations. Thus, when we hear Him, the Exalted, saying that He is One, Unique, or All-Knowing, All-Powerful, Living, Willing, All-Hearing, All-Seeing, or otherwise, we are not to carry these to what is immediately understood by custom regarding the instances, but rather to what His own speech explains and what profound contemplation reveals of their meanings. We have fully addressed this discussion in the discourse on the Clear and the Ambiguous in the third volume of this book.

Other interpretations of the verse have appeared in the narrations and the words of the commentators; we mention those we have come across, and let what we have presented be the first of them.

The second view: That the intended meaning is the heavens of Paradise and the Fire and their earth—meaning what shades them and what carries them. For everything that is above you and shades you is a heaven (سماء), and that upon which your foot rests is an earth (أرض). In other words, what is meant by them is what is above them and what is below them.

This is the view mentioned by al-Zamakhshari at the end of what we quoted from him earlier, and you have seen the problem with it. Furthermore, this view does not suffice to explain the reason for mentioning "the heavens" in the verse in the plural form, as previously mentioned.

The third view: That the intended meaning is "as long as the Hereafter endures," and it endures forever, just as the duration of the heaven and the earth in the world is the measure of the period of its remaining. Perhaps the meaning is that His saying: "as long as the heavens and the earth endure" is placed as a comparison (التشبيه), like your saying: "I spoke to him the speaking of a mocker," meaning like the speaking of one who mocks.

The problem with this is: If comparison were intended by that as we mentioned, it would signify the opposite of the objective—namely, cessation (الانقطاع). And if something else were intended, that phrasing would not suffice for it.

The fourth view: That the intended meaning is to express remoteness (التبعيد) and to signify eternity (الأبدية), not that the intended meaning is the definition by the period of the remaining of the heavens and the earth themselves. For the Arabs have many expressions they use to signify eternity without intending the literal meanings beneath those words, such as their saying: "The matter is such and such as long as night and day differ," "as long as the sun rises," "as long as a star appears," "as long as a breeze blows," and "as long as the heavens endure." They resorted to these and their likes thinking that these things are permanent and will never perish, then they used them as if they were coined for expressing remoteness.

The problem with this is: They only used them for eternity and did so frequently because they thought these matters were permanent and eternal. As for the One who explicitly states in His speech that they are of a timed existence, interrupted, and perishing, and considers belief in that to be one of the obligations of the souls, it is not fitting for Him to place them in speech in the position of eternity in whatever form it is conceived. How could it be so? For the Exalted has said: **ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — *We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a stated term.* (46:3). How can it be correct, along with that, to say that Paradise and the Fire are eternal forever "as long as the heavens and the earth endure"?

**The fifth view:** That the intended meaning is that they are immortal for the duration of the remaining of the heavens and the earth—whose cessation is known—then Allah, the Glorified, increases them beyond that, making them immortal and their stay eternal. This is like saying: "They are immortal for such and such years," then the Exalted adds to that an infinite amount of time, as is said regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لابِثِينَ فِيها أَحْقاباً** — *to remain in them for ages.* (78:23), meaning for ages, then they are increased beyond that.

The problem with this is: It is apparently built upon deriving part of the duration from His saying: "as long as the heavens and the earth endure" and the other part, which is infinite, from His saying: "except what your Lord may wish." Its indication of that depends on assuming matters for which there is no indication in the wording at all.

The sixth view: That the intended meaning by the Fire and Paradise is the Fire and Paradise of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*. These two are immortal as long as the heavens and the earth endure, and when the period of the remaining of the heavens and the earth ends with the onset of the Resurrection, they emerge from them for the final judgment in the arenas of the Gathering.

The problem with this is: It contradicts the context of the verses. For the verses open with the mention of the Day of Resurrection and its description with its specific attributes. It is unlikely that the exposition would begin by mentioning that it is a day on which mankind will be gathered, and that it is a day witnessed, and that it is a day when it comes no soul shall speak except by His leave, until it connects to its most specific and clearest attributes—which is the requital with the eternal Paradise and Fire—only to then turn to mentioning what is in the intermediate realm of Paradise and Fire, which are immortal until the appearance of the Day of Resurrection and are interrupted by it.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, mentions the punishment of the people of the intermediate realm as being "presented" to the Fire, not by "entering" the Fire. The Exalted said: **وَحاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذابِ النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذابِ** — *and a terrible punishment beset Pharaoh’s clan: the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!’* (40:45-46).
