The seventh view: That the intended meaning by entering the Fire is entering into the guardianship (ولاية) of Satan, and by being in Paradise is being in the guardianship of Allah. For the guardianship of Allah is that which appears as Paradise in the Hereafter, in which the happy take delight. And the guardianship of Satan is that which is envisioned in the form of the Fire, thus punishing the criminals on the Day of Resurrection, as signified by the verses indicating the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال).

Thus, the wretched, because of their wretchedness, enter the Fire and perhaps emerge from it if divine care and success reach them—like the disbeliever who believes after his disbelief and the criminal who repents for his crime. And the happy enter Paradise by their happiness and perhaps emerge from it if Satan misleads them and they cling to the earth and follow their desires—like the believer who apostatizes as a disbeliever and the righteous who returns to being wicked.

The problem with this is: What we raised against the previous view—that it contradicts what appears with the aid of the context. For the verses enumerate the exclusive, terrifying, and staggering attributes of the Day of Resurrection, which melt hearts and make minds fly upon hearing them and reflecting on them, in order to warn the people of arrogance and denial among the disbelievers, and to deter the people of disobedience and sins.

It is unlikely that it would be mentioned therein that it is a day on which mankind will be gathered, a day witnessed, and a day when no soul speaks except by His leave, only to then mention that the disbelievers and the people of disobedience are in a Fire since they disbelieved and committed crimes until the Day of Resurrection, and the people of faith and righteous deeds are in a Paradise since they believed and acted righteously. This exposition does not suit the context—firstly—because the verses mention the attributes of the Day of Resurrection specific to it, not what precedes it and leads to it; and—secondly—because the verses are directed toward warning and glad tidings, and these disbelievers and criminals, the people of arrogance and rebellion, do not care for such realities hidden from their senses, nor do they see any value in them, nor are they deterred by fear of such wretchedness or hope for such spiritual happiness, and this is obvious. Yes, it is a correct meaning in itself in the inner depths (باطن) of the Quran.

There are other views here that can be derived from various perspectives in interpreting His saying, the Exalted: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" (except what your Lord may wish). We have omitted mentioning them here for the sake of brevity, because they share with the following views that we will present in interpreting the phrase the same problems that arise against them. So let us suffice with that.

His saying, the Exalted: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" (except what your Lord may wish) is an exception (استثناء) from what preceded it regarding the discourse on immortality in the Fire, and its counterpart is the phrase occurring after the mention of immortality in Paradise. The "ما" in His saying: "ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" is either infinitive (مصدرية), and the estimation—accordingly—is: "except your Lord's wishing their non-immortality." However, this is weakened by His saying afterward: "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ" (Indeed your Lord does whatever He desires), for the "ما" here is relative (موصولة), and the meaning of His saying "ما شاءَ" and His saying "لِما يُرِيدُ" is one.

Or it is relative (موصولة), and the exception is from the period of stay judged as permanent, which is derived from the context. The meaning is: they are immortal in all future successive times except what your Lord wishes of time. Or the exception is from the plural pronoun latent in "immortal" (خالدين), and the meaning is: they are all immortal therein except whom Allah wishes to bring out of it and enter into Paradise. This would be a confirmation of what is in the reports that the sinners and disobedient among the believers do not remain in the Fire forever, but rather emerge from it and enter Paradise eventually through intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah*. For the exit of some from the Fire is sufficient for the invalidation of the general rule and the correctness of the exception.

The discussion remains regarding the application of "ما" in His saying "ما شاءَ" to those who possess intellect (من يعقل), and there is no harm in it, even if it is not common, as it occurs in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: "فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ" (then marry such women as seem good to you) (4:3).

The discussion in the following verse: "وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا" (As for the happy) etc., is similar to the discussion in this verse due to their sharing the same context, except that the exception in the verse of Paradise is followed by His saying: "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" (an endless bounty). Its consequence is that the exception does not point to the actual occurrence (تحقق الوقوع), for it would not suit Paradise being a bounty that is not cut off. Rather, it points to the possibility of occurrence (إمكان الوقوع). The meaning is that the people of Paradise are in it forever, unless Allah were to bring them out of it; however, the gift is permanent, and they are not exiting, and Allah is never wishing that.

Thus, the exception is directed toward establishing the absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) of Allah, and that the power of Allah, the Glorified, is not cut off from them by their entry into the eternal Paradise. His authority does not exhaust, and His kingdom does not vanish or become void. Time is in His hand, and His power and encompassment remain as they were before. So He, the Exalted, has the right to bring them out of Paradise even if He promised them remaining therein forever; however, He, the Exalted, does not bring them out because of His promise, and Allah does not break the promise.

The discussion regarding the exception occurring in this verse—meaning the verse of the Fire—is similar to that in the verse of Paradise due to the unity of context through correspondence and alignment, even though the verse concluded with His saying: "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ" (Indeed your Lord does whatever He desires). In this, there is an indication of actualization that is not hidden.

So the people of immortality in the Fire, like the people of immortality in Paradise, do not emerge from it ever, unless Allah, the Glorified, wishes that, because He is powerful over all things. No act among acts—giving or withholding—causes the loss of His power over its opposite or the departure of the matter from His hand, because His power is absolute, not restricted by one determination over another or by one matter over another. The Exalted said: **وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ** — *and Allah does whatever He wishes.* (14:27); and He said: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ** — *Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes* (13:39), among other verses.

There is no contradiction between this view and what has been narrated in the reports regarding the exit of some criminals from it by the will of Allah, as is not hidden.

This is one view regarding the exception, and there are other views; the author of *Majma' al-Bayan* brought the total to ten, so let what we mentioned be the first of them.

The second: That it is an exception in the increase of punishment for the people of the Fire and the increase of blessing for the people of Paradise. The estimation is: "except what your Lord wishes of increase over this amount," as a man says to another: "I have a thousand dinars against you, except the two thousand that I lent you at such a time." The two thousand are an increase over the thousand without doubt, because the greater is not excepted from the lesser. Based on this, "except" (إِلَّا) would be in the sense of "besides" (سوى), meaning: besides what your Lord wishes, as it is said: "There was no man with us except Zayd," meaning besides Zayd.

The problem with this is: It is built upon His saying: "as long as the heavens and the earth endure" not signifying permanence and eternity, and you have seen the opposite of that.

The third: That the exception applies to their station in the Gathering (المحشر), because they are neither in Paradise nor the Fire, and the period of their being in the intermediate realm (البرزخ), which is what is between death and life. For if He, the Exalted, had said "immortal therein forever" and did not make an exception, the thinker would have thought that they would be in the Fire and Paradise from the time of the revelation of the verse or after the cessation of religious obligation (التكليف); thus, the exception gained a benefit.

If it is asked: How can an exception be made from immortality in the Fire for what precedes entry into it? The answer is: That is permissible if the report of it is before the entry into it.

The problem with this is: There is no evidence for it from the perspective of the wording. Furthermore, this view apparently is built upon His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" signifying deterministic wretchedness and happiness without acquisition (الاكتساب) and choice (الاختيار), and you have seen what is in that.

The fourth: That the first exception is connected to His saying: "لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ" (for them therein is moaning and sighing), and its estimation is: "except what your Lord wishes of the types of punishment outside of these two kinds," and the exception does not relate to immortality. And regarding the people of Paradise, it is connected to what the speech indicates, so it is as if He said: "for them therein is blessing, except what your Lord wishes of the types of blessing," and His saying: "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" only indicated it.

The problem with this is: It is a severance of the connection of the context and its unity without evidence. In it, the first "إِلَّا" is taken in the sense of "besides" (سوى) and the second "إِلَّا" in the sense of exception. Furthermore, there is no clue there for the connection of the first "إِلَّا" to His saying: "لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ," nor does His saying: "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" indicate what he mentioned, for it only indicates the permanence of the gift, not all types of the gift or some of them.

Then, what benefit is there in excepting some types of blessing and manifesting that to the listeners when the station is one of creating desire (التطميع) and glad tidings, and the circumstance is one of invitation and encouragement? This is among the weakest (أسخف) of views.

The fifth: That "إِلَّا" is in the sense of the conjunction "and" (الواو), otherwise the speech would be contradictory. The meaning is: "immortal therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, and what your Lord wishes of increase over that."

The problem with this is: That "إِلَّا" being in the sense of "and" has not been established; it was only mentioned by al-Farra', but they weakened it. Furthermore, the view is built upon the estimation and definition preceding the exception in the two verses not signifying permanence, and you have seen what is in that.

The sixth: That the intended meaning by "those who are wretched" are those entered into the Fire from the people of monotheism (أهل التوحيد) — *those who believe in the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. They are those who joined to their faith and obedience the commission of acts of disobedience that necessitate entry into the Fire. So He, the Glorified, informed that they are punished in the Fire except what your Lord wishes of bringing them out of it to Paradise and delivering the reward of their acts of obedience to them.

As for the exception regarding the people of Paradise, it is an exception from their immortality as well, because whoever is transferred from the Fire to Paradise and remains immortal therein, it is necessary in reporting about him regarding the eternity of his immortality to make an exception for his previous state. So it is as if He said: they are in Paradise, immortal therein, except what your Lord wishes of the time in which He entered them into the Fire.

They said: "those who are wretched" in this view are the very same ones who are "happy"; both descriptions were applied to them in the state that suits that. So when they are entered into the Fire and punished therein, they are people of wretchedness, and when they are entered into Paradise and established therein, they are people of happiness. They attributed this view to Ibn Abbas, Jabir ibn Abdillah, and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri among the Companions, and a group of the Successors.

The problem with this is: It does not suit the context. For He, the Exalted, after mentioning in the description of the Day of Resurrection that it is a day on which mankind will be gathered, divided the people of the gathering into two groups by His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ." It is known that His saying: "فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا" etc., and His saying: "وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا"—both beginning with the "as for" of detail (أما التفصيلية)—are directed toward detailing what was summarized in His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ." The consequence of that is that the intended meaning by "those who are wretched" is all the people of the Fire, not a specific group of them, and the intended meaning by "those who are happy" is all the companions of Paradise, not specifically those brought out of the Fire and entered into Paradise.

Unless it is said: The intended meaning by His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" is also the description of a specific group by their persons, just as the intended meaning by "those who are wretched" and "those who are happy" is one and the same group. The meaning would be that some of the people of the gathering are wretched and happy together; they are those who were entered into the Fire and settled therein, immortal as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord wishes to bring them out of it and enter them into Paradise and make them happy by it, so they remain immortal therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except for an amount of time in which they were wretched, dwelling in the Fire before they entered Paradise.

However, the problem we previously presented then transfers to what was claimed as the meaning of His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ." The apparent context in describing the people of the gathering generally does not support the intention of a specific group among them by His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ"—firstly—and then detailing their state by separating them while they are one and the same group, and presenting them in the form of two subjects for two judgments with two definitions by the duration of the heavens and the earth, then two exceptions whose intended meaning is but one. What benefit is there in this detail other than inheriting ambiguity in the meaning and complexity in the composition?

It is possible to formulate this view on the basis of generalization by saying: The intended meaning by His saying: "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" is a division of the generality of the people of the gathering into the wretched and the happy. The intended meaning by "those who are wretched" is all the people of the Fire, and by "those who are happy" is all the companions of Paradise. The intended meaning by the exception in both places would be the exception of the state of the transgressors among the people of monotheism whom Allah, the Exalted, brings out of the Fire and enters into Paradise. In that case, it would be safe from most of the problems that were raised against the previous view.

The seventh: That the suspension upon the will (المشية) is only by way of emphasizing immortality and making the exit remote, because Allah, the Glorified, does not wish except their immortality according to what He has judged. So it is as if it is a suspension of what will not be upon what will not be, because He does not wish to bring them out of it.

This view shares with the first view the claim that the exception in both instances is not directed toward the invalidation of immortality. However, the first view is specific to the claim that the exception is for the exposition of the absolute divine power, and this view is specific to the claim that the exception is for the exposition that immortality is not invalidated by any cause unless Allah wishes its invalidation, and He will never wish it at all.

This is the point of weakness in it, for His saying "and He will never wish it at all" has no evidence for it. Granted that His saying regarding the people of Paradise: "عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ" suggests or indicates that, but His saying: "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ" does not suggest it nor indicate it, if it does not suggest the opposite, as is apparent.

The eighth: That the intended meaning by it is the exception of the time in which a group of the people of the Fire preceded their entry before another group, and likewise in the groups that enter Paradise. For He, the Exalted, says: **وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَراً** — *The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs... and those who were wary of their Lord will be led to paradise in throngs.* (39:71-73). So a throng among them enters after another throng, and there must occur between them a difference in time, and that is what He, the Exalted, excepts by His saying: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ." This view was narrated from Sallam ibn al-Mustanir al-Basri.

The problem with this is: What is apparent from His saying: "فَفِي النَّارِ خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ" and likewise in His saying: "فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ" etc., is that the description is looking at the period of being in the Fire or Paradise from the perspective of the end (النهاية), not from the perspective of the beginning (البداية).

Furthermore, the starting point for stability in the Fire or in Paradise in any case is the Day of Resurrection, and the state does not differ in that regard from the perspective of the entry of one throng after another and the temporal difference resulting from that.

The ninth: That the meaning is their being immortal in the Fire, punished therein for the duration of their being in the graves as long as the heavens and the earth endure in the world. When they perish and cease to exist, their punishment is interrupted until Allah resurrects them for the reckoning. His saying: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" is an exception that applies to what will be in the Hereafter. This was narrated in *Majma' al-Bayan* from our Shaykh Abu Ja'far al-Tusi in his *Tafsir*, narrating from a group of our companions in their commentaries.

The problem with this is: Its return is to the second view built upon taking "إِلَّا" in the sense of "besides" (سوى) with a difference in the formulation, and you have seen what arises against it.

The tenth: That the intended meaning is "except whom your Lord wishes to pardon, so He does not enter them into the Fire." Thus, the exception is from the pronoun returning to "those who are wretched." The estimation is: "As for those who are wretched, they shall be in the Fire except whom your Lord wishes." It is apparent that this speaker directs the exception in the direction of the people of Paradise—"وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا" until His saying "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ"—by saying that the intended meaning by it is the people of monotheism who emerge from the Fire to Paradise, as preceded in some of the previous views. The meaning is that the happy are in Paradise, immortal therein, except the transgressors among the people of monotheism, for they are in the Fire then they emerge and enter Paradise. This view was attributed to Abu Mijlaz.

The problem with this is: What he mentioned only applies to the first of the two exceptions; the second of the two exceptions must be directed in another way, and whatever it may be, it necessitates the invalidation of the unity of context in the two verses.

Furthermore, the disobedient among the believers whom Allah, the Glorified, pardons so He does not enter them into the Fire from the start are not pardoned haphazardly; rather, they are pardoned for a righteous deed they performed or for intercession. Thus, they become by that "happy" and enter into the second verse: "وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ" etc., without entering into the throng of the wretched and then being excepted for their non-entry into the Fire. In short, they are not wretched so as to be excepted; rather, they are happy, entering Paradise from the beginning.

His saying: "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ" (Indeed your Lord does whatever He desires) is a justification for the exception and a confirmation of the establishment of His power, the Exalted, along with the act in its state of absolute freedom, as preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** — *As for the happy, they will be in paradise. They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty.* (11:108). It was recited as *su'idu* (سعدوا) in the passive voice and as *sa'idu* (سعدوا) in the active voice. The second is more in accord with the language because the root *sa'ada* (سعد) is intransitive in their known usage. However, the first—which is *su'idu* in the passive—along with "شقوا" (were wretched) in the previous verse being in the active voice, is not devoid of a subtle indication that happiness and good are from Allah, the Glorified, and the evil that attaches to them is from themselves, as the Exalted said: **وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً** — *Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure.* (24:21).

*Al-jadh* (الجذ) is cutting, and "عطاء غير مجذوذ" (a bounty not cut off) means not interrupted. His, the Exalted, counting Paradise as a bounty not cut off, despite the preceding exception from immortality by His saying: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ," is among the best evidences that His intention by the exception of the will is to establish the permanence of the absolute nature of His power, and that He is the Master of the affair, never letting its reins depart from His hand.

All the previous similar discussions in the preceding verse apply to this verse, except for those views built upon the excepted one in His saying: "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ" being one who entered the Fire first then emerged from it to Paradise second. This is because it is permissible for some who entered the Fire of the Hereafter to emerge from it, but no one who entered the Paradise of the Hereafter—which is the Paradise of Eternity—as a reward shall ever emerge from it. This is like a necessity from the Book and the Sunnah. The verses and narrations have multiplied regarding that such that one who possesses doubt would not doubt their indication of that, even if the indication of the Book regarding the exit of some who are in the Fire from it is not of that same clarity.

The author of *Majma' al-Bayan* said regarding the necessity of the entry of the people of obedience into Paradise and the impermissibility of their exit from it: "Due to the consensus (الإجماع) of the community that whoever deserves reward must enter Paradise, and that he does not emerge from it after his entry into it." End of quote.

The issue of "the necessity of the entry of the people of reward into Paradise" is built upon an accepted rational principle, which is that fulfilling a promise (الوعد) is obligatory, unlike fulfilling a threat (الوعيد). This is because that which the promise is attached to is a right for the one promised, and not fulfilling it is a waste of the right of another, which is among the forms of injustice (الظلم). As for the threat, it is the establishing of a right for the one who threatens over the transgression for which he threatens. It is not obligatory for the possessor of the right to exact his right; rather, he has the right to exact it and he has the right to waive it. Allah, the Glorified, promised His obedient servants Paradise for their obedience, and threatened the disobedient with the Fire for their disobedience. So it is obligatory that the people of obedience enter Paradise in fulfillment of the right He established for them upon Himself. As for the punishment of the disobedient, it is a right He established for Himself over them, so He has the right to punish them and thus exact His right, and He has the right to leave them by waiving His own right.

As for the issue of not exiting Paradise after entering it, it is something upon which the verses and narrations have multiplied. The consensus he mentioned is built upon what they accepted from the indication of the Book and the Sunnah or reason regarding that, and it is not an independent proof.

## (Commentary on Narrations)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma' wa al-Sifat* narrated from Abu Musa al-Ash'ari who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Indeed Allah, the Glorified, gives respite to the wrongdoer until, when He seizes him, He does not let him escape." Then he recited: **وَكَذلِكَ أَخْذُ رَبِّكَ إِذا أَخَذَ الْقُرى ـ وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ** — *Such is the seizing of your Lord when He seizes the townships while they are wrongdoing. Indeed His seizing is painful and severe.* (11:102).

In the same source: al-Tirmidhi (who graded it as fair), Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab who said: When "فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ" (So some of them are wretched and some are happy) was revealed, I said: "O Messenger of Allah, then upon what do we work? Upon something that has been finished, or upon something that has not been finished?" He said: "Rather, upon something that has been finished and the pens have run with it, O Umar. But everyone is eased (ميسر) toward that for which he was created."

**I say:** This wording is narrated from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله through multiple paths from the paths of the Sunnis—as in *Sahih al-Bukhari* from Imran ibn al-Husayn who said: I said: "O Messenger of Allah, in what do the workers work?" He said: "Everyone is eased toward that for which he was created."

In the same source, also from Ali (may Allah ennoble his face) from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: that he was at a funeral, so he took a stick and began to scratch the ground. He said: "There is not one among you except that his seat in Paradise or the Fire has been written." They said: "Shall we not then rely (نتكل)?" He said: "Work, for everyone is eased toward that for which he was created." And he recited: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى وَاتَّقى** — *As for him who gives and is God-conscious...* (92:5) etc.

To clarify that, we say: It is not hidden from anyone with a modicum of understanding that each of the events occurring in this world—of entities and their effects—as long as it has not donned the garment of actualization and existence, is at the level of possibility (الإمكان). Possibility has a relation to both existence and non-existence together. Thus, wood, as long as it has not become ash by burning, has the possibility of becoming ash and of not becoming so. The sperm, as long as it has not become a human in actuality, has the possibility of humanity—meaning it carries the potential (استعداد) to become a human if the rest of the parts of the necessitating cause (العلة الموجبة) for humanity are gathered with it, and the potential to be nullified and become something other than human.

When it dons the garment of existence and becomes, for example, ash in actuality and a human in actuality, the possibility that related it to ash and others together, and to human and its non-existence together, is nullified. It becomes a human only, its opposite becoming impossible, and ash, its opposite becoming impossible with this actuality.

By this, it becomes clear that if we take the actualities and relate them to their necessitating causes, and likewise relate their causes to the causes of causes, the world from this perspective would be a chain of actualities (فعليات) in which nothing changes from what it is. Possibilities, potentials, and choices (الاختيارات) would all be nullified. And if we look at matters from the perspective of the possibilities and potentials they carry in relation to the goals of their movements, nothing of material things would depart from the realm of possibility and the station of choice.

Thus, existence has two faces: a face of necessity (ضرورة) and actuality in which every part of its parts—of entity or effect of entity—is determined, and it accepts no ambiguity or hesitation, nor any change or substitution. This is the face in which the effects (المسببات) stand by their necessitating causes (أسبابها الموجبة) and the effects (المعلولات) by their complete causes (عللها التامة) which do not separate from their requirements, nor do their effects fail to follow them. No trickery benefits in changing them from what they are, nor does any effort or movement benefit in substituting them.

And another face is the face of possibility (الإمكان) and the form of potential and receptivity (القابلية). According to it, nothing is determined except after occurrence, and it does not emerge from ambiguity and summary except after actualization. Upon this, the law of choice (ناموس الاختيار) stands, and by it, effort and movements are constituted, and work and acquisition are built. To it, teachings, education, fear, hope, wishes, and desires lean. By it, the invitation, command, and prohibition succeed, and reward and punishment are correct.

It is necessary that the two faces do not repel each other in existence, nor does one of them nullify the other. Actuality has its sphere, and possibility and potential have their sphere, just as the ambiguity of such-and-such an event does not repel its determination after actualization, nor does its determination after it repel its ambiguity before it.

The first face is the face of divine decree (القضاء الإلهي). The determination of events according to it does not nullify their lack of determination according to the sphere of invitation, work, and acquisition. We will fully address the discussion on that when we place the discourse on Decree and Destiny (القضاء والقدر) in its appropriate place, if Allah wills.

Let us return to the hadiths:

Reflection on their context reveals that they understood from the writing of happiness and wretchedness, and Paradise and the Fire, and the running of the pen with that, necessity and obligation. They imagined from that, firstly, the necessity of the nullification of the preliminaries leading to the goals, and the lifting of the links between the effects and their causes—and that if Paradise is decreed for a person, it is mandated for him whether he works or does not work, and whether he works righteously or commits evil.

They imagined, secondly, that those preliminaries and causes are counterparts to the goals and effects, falling under the decree, written and mandated; thus, no meaning remains for choice, nor any field for effort and acquisition.

What occurred in the hadiths of their questions, such as their saying: "O Messenger of Allah, then upon what do we work? Upon something that has been finished, or upon something that has not been finished?" and their saying: "O Messenger of Allah, in what do the workers work?" and their saying: "Shall we not then rely?"—meaning shall we not leave work, relying upon what Allah has written in a writing that does not change or substitute?—all of that points to the first imagination. It is as if what they were witnessing in themselves of the attribute of choice and capacity (الاستطاعة) turned them away from pointing to the second of the two imaginations, even if it was entrenched in their hearts, for the two are inseparable.

He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله answered their question by saying: "Everyone is eased toward that for which he was created." This is taken from His saying, the Exalted, in the description of the creation of man: **ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ** — *then He made the way easy for him.* (80:20). Meaning that each of the people of Paradise whom Allah created for it, and of the people of the Fire whom Allah created for it—as He said: **وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** — *Certainly We have winnowed out for hell many of the jinn and humans* (7:179)—has a goal in his creation, and Allah has eased for him the path to that goal and made the traveling of it easy for him.

So between the human for whom Paradise was written and Paradise, there is a path that must be traversed to reach it. And between him and the Fire for which he was written, it is likewise. The path of Paradise is faith and godwariness (التقوى), and the path of the Fire is polytheism and disobedience. So the human for whom Allah wrote Paradise, He only wrote for him the Paradise whose path is faith and godwariness, so he must traverse it. He did not write Paradise for him whether he works or does not work, and whether he works righteously or evilly. Likewise, the one for whom He wrote the Fire, He only wrote for him the Fire through the way of polytheism and disobedience, not absolutely.

Therefore, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله followed his saying: "Everyone is eased toward that for which he was created"—according to the narration of Ali (peace be upon him)—by reciting His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى وَاتَّقى وَصَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى وَأَمَّا مَنْ بَخِلَ وَاسْتَغْنى وَكَذَّبَ بِالْحُسْنى فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرى** — *As for him who gives and is God-conscious, and confirms the best [word], We will surely ease him into facility. But as for him who is stingy and self-sufficing, and denies the best [word], We will surely ease him into hardship.* (92:5-10).

So the one expecting one of the two goals without its path is like the one greedy for satiety without eating, or quenching thirst without drinking, or moving from one place to another without movement. For the abode is but an abode of effort and movement; no goal is attained therein except by traversing and transferring. The Exalted said: **وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى** — *and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward.* (53:39-41).

He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not neglect the answer to the second imagination when he expressed it with "easing" (التيسير). For **easing** is facilitation (التسهيل). It is known that facilitation only actualizes in a matter for which no necessity mandates it, nor does an obligation determine it and close the door of its non-existence. If the path of Paradise were of necessary traversal and mandated crossing absolutely for the human for whom it was written, it would be fixed and unchanging, and there would be no meaning for its easing and the facilitation of its traversal for him, and this is obvious.

So his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, "Everyone is eased toward that for which he was created," indicates that for what the affair of man ends in—of happiness and wretchedness—there are two faces: a face of necessity and mandated decree that does not change from its like path, and a face of possibility and choice eased for the human, which he traverses by work and acquisition. The divine invitation is only directed toward him from the second face, not the first face.

The discussion on determinism (الجبر) and choice (الاختيار) has preceded in the interpretation of His saying: "وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ" (and He misleads none by it except the transgressors) (2:26) in the first volume of the book.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Qatadah: that he recited this verse: "فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا" and said: Anas narrated to us that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "A people will emerge from the Fire," and we do not say as the people of Harura' said.

**I say:** His saying: "and we do not say as the people of Harura' said" is from the words of Qatadah. The people of Harura' are a group of the Kharijites, and they believe in the immortality of whoever enters the Fire therein.

In the same source: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited "فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا" until His saying "إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "If Allah wishes to bring out people—from those who were wretched—from the Fire and enter them into Paradise, He will do so."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Husayn ibn Sa'id al-Ahwazi in the *Kitab al-Zuhd* with his chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the 'Hell-dwellers' (الجهنميين). He said: Abu Ja'far used to say: "They emerge from it, and they are brought to a spring at the gate of Paradise called the Spring of Life (عين الحيوان). Some of its water is sprinkled upon them, and they grow as the crop grows; their flesh, skins, and hair grow."

**I say:** It was also narrated with his chain from Umar ibn Aban from him (peace be upon him). The intended meaning by the 'Hell-dwellers' is a specific group of the people of the Fire, and they are the people of monotheism who emerge from it through intercession, and they are called the 'Hell-dwellers'—not the generality of the people of the Fire, as indicated by what follows.

And in the same source (*al-Burhan*), narrated from him with his chain of transmission from Abu Basir, who said: I heard Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) saying: "Indeed, some people shall come out of the Fire. When they have become like charcoal (حمما), intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — shall reach them." He said: "They will then be taken to a river that flows from the perspiration of the inhabitants of Paradise (مرشح أهل الجنة). They will bathe in it, and their flesh and blood will grow anew, and the grime of the Fire will depart from them. They will enter Paradise, and the inhabitants of Paradise will call them 'the Hell-dwellers' (الجهنميين). Then they will all cry out together: 'O Allah, remove this name from us!' and it will be removed from them." Then he said: "O Abu Basir, indeed the enemies of ‘Ali are the ones who shall abide forever in the Fire, and intercession shall not reach them."

And in the same source, narrated from him with his chain of transmission from ‘Umar ibn Aban, who said: "I heard a righteous servant (عبدا صالحا) saying regarding the Hell-dwellers: 'Indeed, they enter the Fire because of their sins, and they come out by the pardon (عفو) of Allah.'"

And in the same source, narrated from him with his chain of transmission from Humran, who said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Indeed, they say: 'Do you not wonder at a people who claim that Allah will bring a group out of the Fire to place them among the inhabitants of Paradise with the friends of Allah (أولياء الله)?'" He replied: "Do they not read the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He: **وَمِنْ دُونِهِمَا جَنَّتَانِ** 'And besides those two, there are two [other] gardens' (55:62)? It is a garden below a garden, and a fire below a fire. Indeed, they shall not dwell with the friends of Allah." Then he said: "By Allah, between them is a station (منزلة), but I am unable to speak [of it]. Indeed, their matter is tighter than a ring. When the Riser (القائم) — *the divinely appointed leader from the progeny of the Prophet who will rise to establish justice* — rises, he will begin with these [people]."

**I say:** His saying: "When the Riser..." etc., means that when he appears, he will begin with these people who mock the people of truth, taking retribution against them.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Humran from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about His saying: **خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ** 'They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish' (11:107). He said: "This refers to those who come out of the Fire."

And in the same source, narrated from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ** 'Among them are the wretched and the felicitous' (11:105). He said: "In the mention of the inhabitants of the Fire, He made an exception, but in the mention of the inhabitants of Paradise, He did not make an exception: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** 'As for the felicitous, they will be in Paradise, remaining in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty' (11:108)."

**I say:** He (peace be upon him) is pointing out that the exception by the Will (المشية) regarding the inhabitants of Paradise—since Allah followed it with His saying: **عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** 'an endless bounty'—was not an exception indicating the removal of some inhabitants of Paradise from it. Rather, it indicates the absolute nature of Divine Power (إطلاق القدرة). This is in contrast to the exception regarding the inhabitants of the Fire, for it is followed by His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ** 'Indeed your Lord does whatever He desires' (11:107), which suggests the actual occurrence of the act. We have already alluded to this previously.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Suddi regarding His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا** 'As for the wretched...' (11:106) the verse. He said: "Then there came after that [the mention of] the Will of Allah, so He abrogated it and revealed in Madinah: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَظَلَمُوا لَمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقًا** 'Indeed those who disbelieve and do wrong, Allah will never forgive them, nor will He guide them to any way' (4:168) to the end of the verse. Thus, hope was lost for the inhabitants of the Fire to come out of it, and eternal abiding (خلود الأبد) was made mandatory for them." Regarding His saying: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا** 'As for the felicitous...' (11:108) the verse, he said: "Then there came after that [the mention of] the Will of Allah that which abrogated it, and He revealed in Madinah: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ... ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا** 'But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall admit them into gardens... a dense shade' (4:57), thus making eternal abiding mandatory for them."

**I say:** What he mentioned regarding the abrogation (النسخ) — *the replacement of a previous legal ruling or theological implication by a later revelation* — of the two verses, based on his claim that they indicate the cessation [of punishment or reward], you have already seen the evidence to the contrary. Furthermore, abrogation in such instances does not conform to either reason (عقل) or transmitted tradition (نقل). Moreover, this does not agree with the explicit text of the verse regarding Paradise. Additionally, the eternal abiding of both groups is mentioned in many Meccan Surahs, such as *al-An‘am*, *al-A‘raf*, and others.

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-Hasan from ‘Umar, who said: "If the inhabitants of the Fire were to remain in the Fire for a duration equal to the sands of ‘Alij, there would still come a day for them when they would come out of it."

In the same source, Ishaq ibn Rahwayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "A day will come upon Hell when not a single person will remain in it," and he recited: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ شَقُوا** 'As for the wretched...' (11:106).

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ibrahim, who said: "There is no verse in the Quran that gives more hope to the inhabitants of the Fire than this verse: **خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ** 'They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish' (11:107)." He said: And Ibn Mas‘ud said: "A time will surely come upon it when its gates will flap [empty]."

**I say:** What has been mentioned in these three narrations are the opinions of the Companions (الصحابة), and they carry no authority (حجة) over others. Even if they were assumed to be narrations attributed to the Prophet (موقوفة), they would be rejected due to their contradiction with the Book, as the Exalted has said regarding the disbelievers: **وَمَا هُمْ بِخَارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ** 'and they shall not leave the Fire' (2:167).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id in the book *al-Zuhd*, with his chain of transmission from Humran, who said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "It has reached us that a time will come upon Hell when its gates will flap." He replied: "No, by Allah, it is eternal abiding (الخلود)." I said: "**خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ** 'They will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish' (11:107)." He replied: "This refers to those who come out of the Fire."

**I say:** The narrations indicating the eternal abiding of the disbelievers (الكفار) in the Fire from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام) are very numerous. We have already provided a philosophical discussion (بحث فلسفي) on the eternity of punishment and its cessation in the commentary on His saying: **وَمَا هُمْ بِخَارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ** 'and they shall not leave the Fire' (2:167) in the first volume of this book.
